old_claim.note: PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim_absent: No dependency edge is retained because broad DNA sequencing predates PCR and includes non-PCR template preparation routes.

source_shape.source_locator: Chapter 6.1.1, Chain termination DNA sequencing; template routes include plasmid, M13, phagemid, and PCR, followed by the thermal cycle sequencing subsection.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The textbook describes chain-termination sequencing as a mid-1970s method, lists several ways to obtain template DNA, and includes PCR as one possible way to generate template DNA; it separately describes thermal cycle sequencing as similar to PCR.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes a dependency edge: PCR belongs in later PCR-enabled sequencing workflows, not as a prerequisite for the broad DNA sequencing node.

ontology_before: The broad DNA Sequencing node treated PCR as a hard method prerequisite across the whole sequencing scope.
ontology_after: The broad DNA Sequencing node no longer depends on PCR. PCR remains connected downstream through PCR diagnostics and PCR-enabled molecular diagnostic workflows.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant preserved: DNA sequencing remains connected to DNA structure, molecular biology, chemistry, and computing. Changed: PCR is removed from the broad sequencing node and represented only in later PCR-specific workflows.
would_reject_if: A source showed the scoped DNA Sequencing node intentionally means only PCR-amplified sequencing workflows.
A source showed that broad DNA sequencing cannot be performed using cloned templates, M13/phagemid templates, or other non-PCR preparation routes.
The graph split DNA sequencing into PCR-dependent and non-PCR sequencing nodes, requiring the PCR edge only on the narrower PCR-dependent node.
short_reason: PCR is an important sequencing enabler, but broad DNA sequencing predates PCR and includes non-PCR template routes.
